content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 22, 1991


David A. Willoughby
2100 Garden Road, Suite A-210
Monterey, CA 93940

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-429

Dear Mr. Willoughby:

This is in response to your letter requesting assistance on
behalf of the Monterey Peninsula Airport District regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1 You have requested informal assistance.2 Please note
that the Commission's jurisdiction is limited to the provisions of
the Act. Other provisions of the law which may be applicable to
your facts such as Government Code Section 1090 are outside the
jurisdiction of the Commission.3

QUESTION

What are the responsibilities and potential liability of
members of the Monterey Peninsula Airport District and yourself,
as district counsel, where one of the members may be violating the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act?

_____________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear in 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Regulation 18329(c)(1) provides that the Commission may provide
informal assistance to any person with a duty to advise other
persons relating to their duties or actions under the Act.
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

3 Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any
conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is
based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act
as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Odelsby (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-429
Page 2


CONCLUSION

Generally the Act's requirements pertain to individual
officials, not public bodies. Thus, it is the responsibility of
each public official to perform his or her duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by the official's own financial
interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported
the official. However, the Act also provides that it is a
violation of the Act for any person to purposely or negligently
cause any other person to violate the Act, or to aid and abet any
other person in the violation of the Act.

FACTS

You are the counsel for the Monterey Peninsula Airport
District (the "district"). The district is governed by a five-
member board of directors. Your duty as district counsel is to
advise members and employees of the district concerning their
duties under the Act. You stated that the members and employees
of the district are subject to the provisions of the Act and
currently file statements of economic interests.

The district owns and operates the Monterey Peninsula Airport
and leases property at the airport for office and storage space to
private tenants. In 1985, one building was leased to a business
owned and operated by the brother of a member of the board of
directors of the district as an office and for storage needs. The
business subleased a portion of the property to a third party
business for similar uses. The director received no compensation,
nor owned any investment interest in his brother's business.

The long-term lease was initially issued to the member's
brother. However, several years after the lease was entered into,
the lessee was incarcerated and unable to run the business. The
lessee's brother, a director for the district, ran the business on
behalf of his brother without compensation. Since that time the
lessee was released, but he continues to be incapacitated. Thus,
the director continues to run the business without compensation.

DISCUSSION

You have not asked about the liability of the member in
question. Instead, you have asked whether you, or any other
member is liable for the conduct of the member in question, if the
member in question does violate the Act.

Generally the Act's requirements pertain to individual
officials, not public bodies. Thus, it is the responsibility of
each public official to perform his or her duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by the official's own financial
interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported
the official. (Section 81001(b).)

Our File No. I-91-429
Page 3


However, the Act also provides:

Any person who violates any provision of this
title, who purposely or negligently causes any
other person to violate any provision of this
title, or who aids and abets any other person in
the violation of any provision of this title, shall
be liable under the provisions of this chapter.
Provided, however, that this section shall apply
only to persons who have filing or reporting
obligations under this title, or who are
compensated for services involving the planning,
organizing, or directing any activity regulated or
required by this title, and that a violation of
this section shall not constitute an additional
violation under Chapter 11.

Section 83116.5.

Thus, a person who has filing or reporting obligations under
the Act may be liable if he negligently causes another to violate
the Act.4 As counsel for the Monterey Peninsula Airport District,
it would be prudent to advise the members of the district of their
obligations under the Act and caution them to consult the
Commission regarding potential conflicts of interests. Absent
aiding or abetting another person in violating the provision of
the title, members are not responsible for the conduct of other
members.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.5

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWM:aa


4 Please note, the Commission will be considering this issue in
the very near future.

5 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
